artifacts.content:
Hi Sarah,

I’m finally sitting down with a mug of cocoa after we got the kids to bed and finished wiping strawberry goo off every surface in this house. Peak season is officially here.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how we ended up shaping Sweetwater the way we did. Sometimes when I’m out in the berry rows during those quiet weekday u-pick hours, I think back to Dad’s stroke at Lexington Medical Center in October 2019 and those months I spent in the grief support group at Lexington Baptist Church from January through June 2020. Pastor James kept talking about “ordinary holy places,” and honestly, that’s what I wanted this farm to become. That’s why I’m so stubborn about keeping the focus on calm, daytime, family stuff instead of loud nighttime events.

Then Lily’s diagnosis in May 2022 with generalized anxiety and sensory processing issues at Prisma Health Children’s Hospital in Columbia just confirmed it. Dr. Amanda Keller was the one who suggested we add a shaded “calm corner” and more visual schedules. Now when I walk past the little quiet nook behind the farm store and see a kid taking a breather, I feel like all those hard pieces of our story are stitched into something good.

And then there’s you. I know August 2021 and the DUI on Sunset Boulevard and leaving Lexington Middle School nearly broke you. But watching you pour yourself into the Saturday pancake mornings and that little corn maze for Harvest Weekend 2022 has been one of the best parts of this whole “second-chance farm” thing. Customers still ask when the maze is coming back.

Anyway, just wanted to say I see how far we’ve all come. Let’s block a sister breakfast one Monday when the berries slow down.

Love,
Emily

metadata.subject: How all these pieces fit together

artifacts.content:
Hi Ms. Reynolds,

I hope your week is going smoothly and that the class isn’t too sugared up from all the end-of-year treats. I wanted to touch base about the upcoming field trip to Sweetwater Orchard & Creamery and also give you a little context about Lily.

As you know, the farm is also our home place at 1478 Sweetwater Farm Road here in Lexington. My mom, Martha Collins, lives in the original farmhouse and usually has Jacob with her after school while I’m in the office next to the walk-in freezer and online order station. During peak July weekends we can easily have around $18,000 worth of ice cream inventory in there, which still blows my mind when I think about how close it is to the kids’ bedrooms down the hall.

Part of why our field trips are very low-key, strictly daytime, and structured is Lily’s experience. In May 2022 she was diagnosed with generalized anxiety and sensory processing issues by pediatric psychologist Dr. Amanda Keller at Prisma Health Children’s Hospital in Columbia. Since then, we’ve built a small shaded “calm corner” behind the farm store and use simple visual schedules for all school groups, not just hers. We also avoid loud music and sudden announcements in the fields.

For your class, we’ll have:
– A clearly posted picture schedule from bus arrival to departure
– Options for kids to step into the calm corner with an adult if they get overwhelmed
– A quieter picking row set aside if anyone needs less noise

If there’s anything specific that would help Lily or any of her classmates (ear defenders, extra transition warnings, etc.), please let me know. I really want the kids to leave with sticky fingers, happy memories, and not a lot of stress.

Warmly,
Emily Harper
Sweetwater Orchard & Creamery

metadata.subject: Field trip details + a bit about Lily

artifacts.content:
Mark,

Quick brain dump before I forget everything we talked about today.

I appreciate you walking me through where we stand. I still can’t believe how fast things snowballed after we opened the creamery side in March 2021. One minute we were celebrating our first batch of chocolate milk, the next I was staring at that tax delinquency notice from the South Carolina Department of Revenue about being two quarters behind on sales and use tax in late 2022.

Driving up to the SCDOR office in Columbia back in February 2023 to set up that $650/month payment plan was one of the more humbling days of my life. I remember sitting in the parking lot, texting Daniel from my spot between the big glass doors and the “Pay Here” sign, wondering if we’d totally blown it by trying to grow too fast.

What you said today helped: that as long as we keep making those $650 payments on time and stay current on everything going forward, this doesn’t have to define the farm. I liked your phrase about us being a “small, transparent operation” that hit a rough patch but is working through it.

I’m trying to hold that in tension with the rest of our reality: Mom in the cottage out back since Dad’s stroke and passing in October 2019, the kids’ rooms down the hall from the office and walk-in freezer, Sarah rebuilding after her August 2021 DUI on Sunset Boulevard and leaving Lexington Middle School. It’s all so personal that sometimes I forget we’re a business too.

Could you send me a simple one-page snapshot of our current numbers and the payment plan details so Daniel and I can review them together this weekend?

Thanks again,
Emily
Sweetwater Orchard & Creamery

Personal journal – late night reflections

Some days it feels like this whole place is stitched together out of second chances and grief.

Dad’s fatal stroke at Lexington Medical Center in October 2019 is still the fault line everything runs along. I can picture the hospital hallway, the plastic chairs, Mom’s face. Taking over Sweetwater Orchard & Creamery from her in March 2020 felt less like a business transition and more like grabbing the wheel of a truck that was already rolling downhill. The grief support group at Lexington Baptist Church from January–June 2020 kept me from falling apart. Pastor James talking about “making room for lament” is what planted the seed for our quieter weekday u-pick hours and the sensory-friendly mornings in the berry fields.

Then Lily’s diagnosis in May 2022 with generalized anxiety and sensory processing issues at Prisma Health Children’s Hospital in Columbia shifted everything again. Dr. Keller’s suggestions about a shaded calm corner and visual schedules are now literally built into the farm. I see her hand every time a kid sits under that shade sail behind the store.

And Sarah. August 2021 on Sunset Boulevard in Lexington, the DUI, the call from Mr. Hayes at Lexington Middle School about her leaving her teaching job. I remember watching her stand in the berry field that first weekend we brought her on part-time, hands shaking as she poured batter for what would become our Saturday morning pancake menu. By Harvest Weekend 2022, she’d sketched the little corn maze on a napkin and turned it into something families still ask about.

Sometimes I worry the farm is carrying too much of our story. Other times, when I watch a family pick berries in silence together, it feels exactly right.

metadata.title: Why the farm feels so heavy (and holy)

artifacts.content:
Event: Tax payment + errands in Columbia

Date: Tuesday, February 14, 2023
Time: 9:00 a.m. – 2:30 p.m.
Location: Columbia – SCDOR office + Prisma Children’s + Costco

Details / To‑dos:

– 9:00–10:30 a.m. South Carolina Department of Revenue office (Columbia)
  • Meet with rep about formalizing the $650/month payment plan for the two quarters we fell behind on sales and use tax in late 2022.
  • Bring: folder from our accountant, Mark Ellison, with all returns; checkbook; current profit/loss from Sweetwater Orchard & Creamery.
  • Questions: confirm auto‑draft dates; ask what happens if a month falls during peak July weekends when we’re sitting on ~$18,000 of ice cream inventory in the walk‑in freezer.

– 10:45 a.m.–12:00 p.m. Swing by Prisma Health Children’s Hospital in Columbia
  • Drop off updated intake forms for Lily with Dr. Amanda Keller’s office (follow‑up on her May 2022 generalized anxiety + sensory processing diagnosis).
  • Ask about any resources for parents running small family businesses / farms.

– 12:00–1:30 p.m. Costco run
  • Bulk sugar, flour, pancake mix for Sarah’s Saturday morning pancake menu.
  • Extra paper goods for upcoming Harvest Weekend 2023 planning (think back to how fast we ran out during Harvest Weekend 2022 with the trial corn maze).

– 1:30–2:30 p.m. Drive back to 1478 Sweetwater Farm Road
  • Check in with Mom (Martha) in the farmhouse about Jacob’s after‑school pickup.
  • Quick walk around berry fields if time/energy.

Thread with: “Church Girls” group chat (Me, Hannah, Kelsey)
Date: 2022-09-18, evening

Emily: Y’all, I just got the kids down and I’m hiding in the laundry room with a bowl of blackberry cobbler.

Hannah: Honestly that sounds like self‑care 😂 How was Harvest Weekend round 2?

Emily: Good but exhausting. The little corn maze that Sarah dreamed up for Harvest Weekend 2022 was a HUGE hit. I’m proud of her. Hard to believe it’s only been a year since the DUI mess on Sunset Boulevard and her leaving Lexington Middle School. Seeing her out there in the maze with the kids… it felt like redemption.

Kelsey: I’m so glad she has Sweetwater. How’s she really doing?

Emily: Better. Still fragile. But she poured herself into the Saturday pancake mornings and that maze. People kept asking if we’re going to do a bigger one next year.

Emily: It’s weird how much of our family story is baked into this place. Dad’s stroke at Lexington Medical Center back in Oct 2019, my months in that grief support group at Lexington Baptist Church, then Lily’s diagnosis in May 2022 at Prisma Children’s with Dr. Keller. No wonder I’m obsessed with quiet mornings and the calm corner behind the store.

Hannah: You’ve turned a lot of pain into something beautiful, Em.

Emily: Some days it feels beautiful. Some days it feels like too much. Also, practical question: any of you have a good slow‑cooker recipe for church next Sunday that doesn’t use cream? We’re drowning in milk from the creamery but I promised Daniel I’d actually bring something that’s not dairy‑heavy for once 😂

Kelsey: I’ve got you. Sending a link.

Hi Daniel,

Figured I’d write this out while it’s quiet so we’re not trying to have a serious conversation over dishes and spelling words tonight.

I keep circling back to what Sweetwater Orchard & Creamery actually is for us right now. We took it over from Mom in March 2020 with all of Dad’s October 2019 Lexington Medical Center memories still raw, then opened the creamery in March 2021, then stumbled into being two quarters behind on sales and use tax in late 2022. Sitting in the South Carolina Department of Revenue office in Columbia this February, hashing out that $650/month payment plan, I kept thinking, “This is our home we’re betting on.”

Because it really is our home: 1478 Sweetwater Farm Road, kids’ bedrooms down the hall from the office and the walk‑in freezer that can hold around $18,000 in ice cream on those peak July weekends. Mom in the cottage, Jacob wandering between her kitchen and ours, Lily pacing the hallway when her anxiety spikes.

Lily’s May 2022 diagnosis at Prisma Health Children’s Hospital with Dr. Keller is still shaping so many decisions. I’m glad we’ve leaned into the sensory‑friendly mornings, the calm corner, the visual schedules. It lines up with what I felt in that Lexington Baptist grief group back in 2020—that the farm should be a peaceful place.

And then there’s Sarah, rebuilding after the August 2021 DUI on Sunset Boulevard and leaving Lexington Middle. Her Saturday pancake menu and that Harvest Weekend 2022 corn maze are now things customers identify us with. “The pancake farm,” they keep saying.

Where I’m going with this: when we talk about adding the shade structures and maybe that little classroom, I want us to keep all of this in mind. We’re not just expanding a business; we’re deepening the place that’s been holding our whole messy story.

Let’s look at numbers with Mark this week, but also maybe take a walk around the pond and ask if it still feels like the life we chose.

Love,
Em
